# Transcrição: 1-Strategy With Frank Kern

- **Fonte:** 1-Strategy With Frank Kern
- **Duração:** 1:07:07
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:02]

Hey everybody, Frank Kern here and welcome to this very carefully scripted and professional video interview with myself and the lovely and talented Tom Breeze. Thank you very much Frank. All the way from the country of Europe today. Tom has flown in from your capital, Europe City. Yep. It was a very nice place, very lovely. They have Europe land there, which is a really fabulous attraction. It's great. There are great food, huge portions of their food over there. And today what I figured out, we would do is I would show you some behind the scenes stuff of what is working now. Because Tom and I are working together to make a considerable amount of money. And when I say we're working together, what I really mean is I'm doing basically nothing. I do everything. You just sit back. Yes. Look who's getting the credit right now. This is great. So I have single handedly made this work. And Tom is fortunate enough to just sit back and do all the work that has powered it. That's the way it works. So how about I show them what we're sending traffic to and then I'll ask you how it all actually works. I know how it works, but you know how these Europeans are. You know what I mean? They get really fussy on camera. So what do you do? I'll let you do the self introduction. I'll stop yapping for a moment. Okay. Well basically what we do is YouTube advertising. So I run an agency and we use YouTube ads like Pre-Row ads and in-display ads which are now called discovery ads. But basically any video or any ad that you can run on YouTube, we make sure we get in front of a good quality audience and then send it back to a web page of choice. In this case, it's going to be the book funnel, right? It is in fact going to be the book funnel. And Sastres, does he look at the red one or the one beside the red one when he's a... Left one. All right. So look at the left one. Left one. That one there. So guys, we've got three cameras here. Actually there's another one somewhere. Oh, there's one right there too. There's all kinds of cameras that you never know which one to look at. So we don't have lazy eyes or anything. So what are we spending per day right now on the Google traffic? I think we're only about 1,500ish per day. Well we're just small potatoes here. But we're starting to... Thanks a lot. I thought we were pretty cool. The Sergey brand was about to fly me up to Google headquarters and be like, Frank, this is serious business. Our stocks have gone like this because of your spend. So we're scaling up pretty good. And I thought we were about to go up to 2,500 or something. I think we're making more money than I thought. Yeah, we can go easily that way. This is great. I thought we were spending more, which means our ROI is higher. Okay, so we're at about 1,500 bucks a day, which is lovely. Last month between Google, and I consider YouTube and everything Google. So I'm going to say Google a lot. The stuff he's talking about, it's not like your typical AdWords and AdSense stuff. We'll get into the mechanics of what's working in a minute. But I'll give you last month's numbers, which I have on my phone because I'm just that ADD. So this is for the month of October.

## [03:02 - 06:07]

So the book funnel, which I'm going to walk you through, you might have seen this funnel before, so I'll just show you mechanically how it works. The funnel itself brought in $100,736. So $100,736 in collected revenue. So that means money that has been gotten out. Some people who go through the book funnel buy things on an installment plan, like a three-pay or a two-pay. So we have within 30 days another $30,885 coming in, and then in month two, there's another $6,984 coming in. So if you were to use internet math, it would be $138,000. But I don't really want to count the receivables until they come in because internet marketing math is often a fantasy. So I don't think all of that money will come in. See people on an installment plan, there will be some percentage of breakage or falloff, which is fine. That's cool. Now we spent $51,496 on the Facebook, and we just started scaling Google. And I have your answer here, my good man, somewhere on what we spent on Google or YouTube. We spent $29,319.44. So we're at about a grand a day, but now we're scaling right back up, which is good. So where are we sending the traffic, and how are we doing it? Well I am doing nothing because I've got sense enough to let smart people actually do it. So I write check. This is a classic example of dumb guy giving check to smart guy and benefiting from smart guy's experience, which is wonderful. So I'll walk him through the funnel, and then we'll show him how the traffic is working. I gotta tell you, this is working better for me than Facebook, incidentally. I get a higher ROI from YouTube, Google, than Facebook, which is wonderful. So this is the loss leader funnel, also known as the book funnel. This might seem familiar to you, I've covered it before. So what we do is we start out with some of them ads. So you see a copy of the Facebook ad and the YouTube ad here. So the YouTube ad is the one on the right, and we will show them, I think, some of the YouTube ads as well. We've got them already. And so this little sequence is if they're already on my list, but in this case, these guys are going directly from this ad here to my webpage. So you see kind of a little screenshot of the webpage. And a lot of you guys have probably seen this webpage. This is about a 10-page long form sales letter selling a book for $5.60. So if they go from the ad and they buy the book, which is really the only thing they can do from the ad, they are a series of two one-click upsells. One is for a product called ReFrame, and it's $297. If they say no to that, I'll offer it to them for three payments of $97. And regardless of whether they say yes or no to either of those, there's the second upsell, which is for Video Blackbox, which is for two payments of $97. That is why we have some receivables in the upcoming two months, because some people took the two-pay, and some people bought Video Blackbox, which is awesome. So the thank you page for all of this, whether they buy or not, is the webinar registration

## [06:07 - 09:08]

page. The second webinar, which sells my client acquisition system at the discounted price of $1,860. And that is the entire funnel. So I think, yeah, so here's the mechanics of the email and stuff. I'm not sure if I've got, these are my emails, and guys, I'm going through this quickly because I've sent you this before in a prior month, I think. Yeah, so here's a screenshot of the long form sales letter, and here are what the web pages look like. So there's my sales letter, and this is what the two upsells look like. And that is what my webinar registration page looks like. So it is very, very basic. So the purpose of today's video isn't really to walk you through the funnel itself, and I'll get this off the screen for you so you can pull up the traffic and stuff. But it's to tell you how I'm getting traffic to it, because I've talked about the funnel before, so I don't want to be too redundant there, because I've sent you that in a past issue. So how are you making all of this magic happen? Where do we want to start? I'll kind of shut up and let you do that. Okay, cool. So when it comes to YouTube, and it's good that you mentioned Facebook, I think, as well, because I think that it's good to try and do like for like comparison. I think with Facebook, people go to Facebook because they're in a social kind of place and they want to connect to people, communicate with people. And we know they're interested in what you may have to offer, because they've liked certain pages and so on and so forth. With YouTube, we not only know that they are interested and they like what you might be up to, but we also know they're in a motivated state. So they're actually looking to go and find out more information about knowing how to do something, wanting to go and buy something, wanting to learn some more information about something. And so they're really in a kind of what we call an active intent place. So they're kind of like actually looking and motivated to take the next step, which makes the closing or makes the kind of next step for them a logical next step, because they want to find out more information. And we don't have to do a huge sales pitch or anything like that. And that's why I think that the ROI of YouTube versus Facebook tends to be better through the funnel, which can cost a little bit more to get a book sale or to get an opt-in from YouTube. But once they do convert, they tend to give you more money as they go through the funnel. What's good is we've had lower cost to acquire on YouTube. So I'm spending less to get, it cost me $16 and a couple pennies on average for October's numbers, to get a book buyer on YouTube. So I'm spending $16 and change. You get a $5 and 60 cent book buyer. I'm making the money, of course, in the one-click up sales and the automated webinar sales. So we spent a total of 80 something thousand between the two media platforms. We got 100,000 up front immediately. So we got about a 25% return on the investment immediately. So I really, I'm reiterating that because people want me to go and do your crazy paying $16 and change. You get a $5 and 60 cent book. But when we go just to buyers and it's doing better for us than Facebook, which is great because I'm like $23 a buyer on the Facebooks. Interesting. Yeah. Well, I think you hit the nail on the head. Facebook, you're interrupting them.

## [09:08 - 12:10]

They're like, oh, I'm going to argue about politics with this guy that I don't know. Oh, there's an ad for this peckerhead with the book. And we're on YouTube. And what you're doing, they're typing in how to get clients and things. Yeah, exactly. How to market my business, how to go and do this. But we know they're in that mode where they've got up their bum to begin with and they're actually out there searching for information. And so we can get in front of them there. So there's different targeting options which we'll go through. But it means that we can get in front of a really hot kind of audience that are so much closer to making a purchasing decision. We might be able to find another screen grab. I guess it was a bad angle. So let's talk about like pre-roll ads to begin with, which are the ads that will come up before the video you're about to watch. So someone's like looking up a video. We know they might be very interested in what the audience is looking for and we say, great, we're going to put our ad in front of that video. The great thing about these video ads is that they'll run. People can obviously press the skip ad button if they wish, and that means they'll disappear. But you'll only ever pay on these ads when someone either clicks to go back to your website or watches past 30 seconds, unless your video is less than 30 seconds. So it gets to the end of your video and that's prior to 30 seconds, you'll pay there as well. But what you're doing here is you're paying per engagement and it's called the, not pay-per-view, I can't remember what it's called now. We could make it up. Yeah, it'll come to me in a second. The other paper. Trueview. I think we'll be square. Trueview. It's called Trueview. Pay-per-view 2000. So you're only paying for a true view, which means really you're only paying for someone who's actually engaged with your video ad. So the options for people really are to click this button, like button in the bottom left

Sometimes we can create videos that are like 20 seconds in length and it does the job. I would say that depends on what you're selling at the end of it, if you're going to be promoting a free opt-in for something, which is easy for someone to take up, say for example, it's a cheat sheet, for example, you can probably do that pretty quick. Like a quite short video because it's like, if it makes sense for someone, I'll get out and grab that straight away as an instant return. If you're asking someone to opt-in for a webinar, you might need a little bit longer because that's quite a mental commitment from somebody to be like, okay, I'm going to give you an hour of my time in a future date. So you might want to spend a bit more time selling the webinar. If it's a book sale, not even asking for opt-in, then I'd say that you probably do want to spend a bit more time on the actual video itself. Therefore it was intentional. I mean, actually it was pretty hard making these videos because it's hard to keep a straight face in everything. We joke around and hear a pretty good bit. All right, so I think really the short answer is just make the damn thing and see what happens because you don't actually have to spend a ton of money to scale out. Like when we first started, you were spending 50 bucks a day or something like that. I couldn't even be less than that. Sometimes campaigns that we'll start out with will be like 10, 20 bucks a day just because we don't want it to go crazy.

## [18:11 - 21:11]

And it's kind of like you can spend a lot of money on YouTube quickly because there's a lot of videos that you can advertise on. So it's a case of finding out what targeting is going to work for you, spend a bit of money, find out what's working and then simply just start scaling. And you won't have the same problem that you get with Facebook where as soon as you start scaling, all of a sudden it can go haywire sometimes or you run out of, like you get the ad fatigue or you start running out of the amount of advertising you can do. And as a result, the cost goes up. With YouTube, remember it's like that. It's almost like a conveyer of new people searching for information.
